  1. Therapeutic Areas
  2. Immunology

Immunology

Therapeutic Area Guide for Real World Analytics & Accelerators (RWAA)

Published

April 6, 2026

Why Immunology Matters to RWAA

Immunology is one of Lilly’s most dynamic and rapidly expanding Therapeutic Areas, encompassing treatments for immune-mediated inflammatory diseases (IMIDs) including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, axial spondyloarthritis, psoriasis, atopic dermatitis, and inflammatory bowel disease. These conditions share dysregulated immune pathways and frequently overlap in patient populations, creating rich opportunities for cross-indication real-world research.

RWAA supports Immunology across the full evidence lifecycle — from characterizing treatment patterns in a highly competitive biologic market, through comparative effectiveness and safety studies, to generating evidence that informs payer and HTA decisions in an increasingly crowded therapeutic landscape.

Disease Areas & RWD Landscape

Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)

RA is among the most mature disease areas in real-world data, with well-established disease registries, validated claims-based algorithms, and decades of longitudinal data available. The competitive biologic and JAK inhibitor market makes treatment sequencing and switching analyses particularly high-value.

Key RWD considerations:

  • Claims data (Optum, MarketScan, IQVIA): Strong for treatment patterns, biologic cycling, adherence, and HCRU
  • EHR data (Optum EHR, TriNetX): Adds lab values (CRP, ESR, RF, anti-CCP) and disease activity proxies
  • Specialty registries (CORRONA/Adelphi): Clinical depth including DAS28, HAQ scores, and patient-reported outcomes
  • Linkage opportunities: Registry-claims linkages provide the richest evidence base for CER in RA

Psoriasis & Psoriatic Arthritis (PsO / PsA)

Psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis frequently co-occur and share treatment pathways, making joint analyses common. Dermatology and rheumatology data sources differ, and studies often need to span both specialties.

Key RWD considerations:

  • Diagnosis code algorithms for PsO are reliable in claims; PsA requires additional specificity criteria
  • Skin clearance outcomes (PASI, IGA) are not captured in claims — EHR or registry data required
  • Joint disease activity in PsA (tender/swollen joint counts) requires specialty registry or structured EHR
  • Biologic sequencing analyses are high-priority given multiple approved IL-17, IL-23, and TNF inhibitors

Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA)

axSpA, including ankylosing spondylitis (AS) and non-radiographic axSpA (nr-axSpA), presents unique RWD challenges due to diagnostic delays and undercoding of nr-axSpA in administrative data.

Key RWD considerations:

  • AS is well-coded in claims (ICD-10: M45); nr-axSpA is significantly undercoded and hard to identify without imaging data
  • Symptom onset-to-diagnosis delay (often 7–10 years) complicates cohort construction and washout design
  • MRI findings, HLA-B27 status, and BASDAI/ASDAS scores require registry or structured EHR linkage
  • Time-to-diagnosis and healthcare journey analyses are high-value for this indication

Atopic Dermatitis (AD)

AD is a growing priority within Immunology, with increasing biologic and JAK inhibitor options entering the market. RWD in AD is less mature than in RA, and endpoint ascertainment presents unique challenges.

Key RWD considerations:

  • Claims-based AD cohort identification is reliable (ICD-10: L20); moderate-to-severe classification requires additional proxies (e.g., systemic therapy use, specialist visits)
  • Skin outcome measures (EASI, IGA, SCORAD) are not available in claims — registry or EHR required
  • Itch and sleep disruption PROs are important for patient-centered evidence; require survey or registry linkage
  • Dupilumab entry has reshaped the treatment landscape — biologic naïve vs. experienced analyses are common

Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD)

IBD — encompassing Crohn’s disease (CD) and ulcerative colitis (UC) — is a complex area with high unmet need, frequent treatment cycling, and growing regulatory interest in RWE to support label expansions.

Key RWD considerations:

  • CD and UC are reliably identified in claims (ICD-10: K50, K51); disease severity proxies require hospitalization, steroid use, or biologic history
  • Endoscopic outcomes (mucosal healing) are not available in claims — EHR or registry linkage needed
  • Fistulizing CD and extraintestinal manifestations require additional code-based algorithms with validation
  • Biosimilar uptake and switching analyses are increasingly requested

Common RWAA Study Types in Immunology

Common Immunology Study Types
Study Type Typical Requestor Data Source(s) Notes
Treatment pattern analysis Medical Affairs, HEOR Claims, EHR Biologic cycling, switching, persistence, LOT
Comparative effectiveness (CER) HEOR, HTA Claims, EHR, registry Active comparator new-user design preferred
Safety/pharmacovigilance GPS Claims, EHR, registries Infections, malignancies, CV events
Unmet need / disease burden Pipeline teams, Medical Affairs Claims, EHR Supports label expansion or pipeline asset
Budget impact / cost analyses Market Access, HEOR Claims Payer-facing; may feed HTA dossiers
PRO / patient experience HEOR Survey, registry Disease activity, QoL, itch, fatigue
Real-world effectiveness (RWE) Regulatory, HEOR Claims, EHR Increasing regulatory use; requires protocol rigor
Biologic sequencing / LOT Medical Affairs, Market Access Claims High demand given crowded competitive landscape

Key Data Sources for Immunology

Claims Databases

  • Optum Clinformatics — large commercially insured + Medicare Advantage population; good for HCRU and biologic utilization
  • IBM MarketScan — employer-sponsored insurance; well-suited for working-age RA, PsO, and AD populations
  • IQVIA PharMetrics Plus — broad commercial; strong for specialty pharmacy linkage and biologic dispensing
  • CMS Medicare / Medicaid — important for elderly RA patients and Medicaid-covered IBD populations

EHR Databases

  • Optum EHR — includes labs (CRP, ESR), vital signs, and some structured disease activity data
  • TriNetX — federated EHR network; useful for feasibility, cohort sizing, and rapid turnaround studies
  • Veeva Iqvia — structured EHR with specialty focus; useful for rheumatology and dermatology practices

Specialty Registries

  • CORRONA (Adelphi) RA Registry — gold standard for RA RWD; includes DAS28, HAQ, and biologic history
  • CORRONA PsA/SpA Registry — clinical depth for joint disease activity and biologic sequencing
  • CorEvitas AD Registry — growing registry with EASI, IGA, PROs for atopic dermatitis
  • TREAT Registry (Crohn’s disease) — long-term CD outcomes with endoscopic and clinical data

Specialty / Linked Sources

  • Specialty Pharmacy Data — critical for biologics dispensed outside retail; Accredo, CVS Specialty
  • Linked Claims + Registry — preferred for CER when clinical endpoints (disease activity scores) are needed alongside utilization
Data Source Selection Tip

Immunology studies frequently require disease activity scores and clinical endpoints not available in claims. When these are primary endpoints (e.g., DAS28 remission, PASI 90, mucosal healing), specialty registries or linked EHR sources are required. Claims alone are sufficient for treatment patterns, HCRU, and event-based safety endpoints.

Analytic Considerations

Cohort Design

  • New-user active comparator design is the gold standard for CER across all Immunology indications; reduces channeling bias in a market with many available biologics
  • Biologic-naïve vs. biologic-experienced analyses are often requested separately given differences in prognosis and treatment response
  • Line of therapy (LOT) construction requires careful rules for gaps in therapy, switching, and add-on therapies
  • Washout periods should account for long half-lives of biologic therapies (typically 6–12 months)

Confounding Control

  • Immunology populations have complex channeling — disease severity, prior biologic history, comorbidities, and prescriber specialty all drive treatment selection
  • Propensity score methods (IPTW, matching) are standard; include biologic history and disease severity proxies as key covariates
  • Prescriber-level clustering should be accounted for (biologics are specialty-prescribed; practice patterns vary)

Endpoint Ascertainment

  • Infection endpoints (serious infections, opportunistic infections, herpes zoster) are high-priority for JAK inhibitor safety studies; validated claims algorithms exist
  • Malignancy endpoints require extended follow-up and cancer registry linkage where possible
  • Joint replacement surgery is a useful long-term disease progression endpoint in RA and PsA — well-captured in claims
  • Flare ascertainment (steroid bursts, ER visits, hospitalizations) is the most common claims-based disease activity proxy

Regulatory-Grade RWE

Immunology is seeing growing regulatory interest in RWE, particularly for:

  • Post-market safety commitments (JAK inhibitors — ORAL Surveillance follow-on studies)
  • Label expansion support for new indications (e.g., pediatric populations, new IMIDs)
  • Pre-specified SAPs and protocol registration are required for regulatory-grade studies

RWAA’s Role Across Immunology Functions

Key Collaborators & Contacts

Function Typical Ask to RWAA Notes
HEOR Disease burden, CER inputs, PRO linkage Closest collaboration; often co-lead studies
HTA Comparative effectiveness, NMA data inputs High demand given crowded IL-17, IL-23, JAK market
GPS Safety surveillance, infection and malignancy monitoring JAK inhibitor safety is a standing priority
Medical Affairs Treatment patterns, biologic sequencing, unmet need Often publication-oriented
Market Access Payer analyses, formulary support Competitive positioning vs. biosimilars and new entrants
Regulatory RWE study design and execution Post-market commitments for JAK inhibitors
